APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — this is squarely an NLP research contribution: it targets event factuality (a semantic/IE problem) and proposes neural architectures (BiLSTM+CNN with attention) evaluated on the standard FactBank corpus with 5-fold cross-validation and standard P/R/F1 metrics (Section 5). The paper situates itself against relevant prior work (Saurí’s rule system, de Marneffe’s feature models) and uses methods and evaluation practices expected at top NLP/ML venues, so it is an appropriate fit for the conference audience.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses a clearly important problem—event factuality—which the authors argue is useful for many downstream tasks (opinion detection, QA, textual entailment, rumor identification) and is evaluated on the standard FactBank dataset (Introduction; 5.1). Its main contributions (a two-step supervised framework, an attention-based CNN to detect Source Introducing Predicates, and a BiLSTM+CNN factuality model) adapt powerful neural techniques to a relatively under-explored annotation task and are shown to outperform rule- and feature-based baselines (contributions; experimentation). However, the work mainly applies existing neural components to a specific subproblem with evaluation confined to FactBank, so while likely to be adopted by researchers working on factuality and related NLP pipelines, it is a moderate (not transformative) advance with limited immediate broader impact.

CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is organized in the standard order (Introduction → Method → Experiments → Related Work) and the three main contributions are stated clearly and early in the Introduction. Notation is mostly defined (LSTM equations, Hp, H etc.) and examples/figures (e.g. the dependency-path example in Figure 4 and Table 2) are informative. However, the manuscript contains numerous duplicated/garbled passages and formatting artifacts (repeated paragraphs, stray LaTeX notes and repeated citations) that make parts of the text hard to follow and harm readability; a careful cleanup and proofreading would substantially improve clarity.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies existing neural components (BiLSTM, CNN and attention) to event factuality and proposes a two-step pipeline plus task-specific inputs (SIP_Path, RS_Path, Cue_Path, Aux_Words) and an attention-based CNN for SIP detection (see contributions and Section 4). These ideas adapt prior work on syntactic-path modeling and attention (e.g. Xu et al., Roth & Lapata) rather than introducing fundamentally new architectures or learning paradigms, so the contribution is an informed, task-focused adaptation rather than a breakthrough. The manuscript clearly differentiates itself from earlier rule-based and feature-based systems (Saurí, de Marneffe) but the technical novelty is moderate (incremental).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines on the same dataset (FactBank) and evaluation metrics: it includes the strong rule-based system of Saurí (2008), a MaxEnt model using de Marneffe et al. (2012) features, and a CNN ablation. Comparisons appear fair (5-fold CV, macro/micro F1, significance testing) and the authors explicitly note limitations (some MaxEnt features unavailable because of their factor-extraction setup) and show ablation analyses of inputs. Minor shortcomings: some older prior systems (e.g., Diab/Prabhakaran) are only discussed rather than included as baselines, and the MaxEnt baseline is constrained by missing features which could slightly bias the comparison.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are adequate but limited: the model is evaluated on FactBank with 5-fold CV and standard baselines (Rules, MaxEnt, CNN+CNN) and reports macro/micro F1 (Section 5.1), and the paper presents meaningful ablations on inputs (Figure 6) and a CNN+CNN variant comparison. Related work coverage is thorough (Section 6 cites major prior systems and neural approaches). However, there is no discussion of failure cases or limitations, the evaluation is confined to a single dataset (no cross-domain or robustness tests), and reproducibility is only partially supported — some hyperparameters and Word2Vec initialization are given but key details (parser used, learning rates, training epochs/batch size, exact preprocessing/splits) are missing.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (two-step pipeline, attention-based SIP detector, BiLSTM+CNN model) and provides experiments on FactBank with 5-fold CV, reasonable baselines (rule-based, MaxEnt, CNN variant) and standard LSTM/attention equations that are correct. However, the provided text omits full numerical results for the main comparisons, and the experimental description has gaps (e.g. using a two-sample t-test rather than a paired test on CV folds, incomplete discussion of hyperparameter selection and the impact of errors from the earlier factor-extraction pipeline). These issues do not prove the approach wrong but raise methodological concerns that limit confidence in the claimed gains.
